statement_lock.original_statement:
For which limit ordinals $\alpha$ is it true that if $G$ is a graph with vertex set $\alpha$ then $G$ must have either an infinite path or independent set on a set of vertices with order type $\alpha$?




A problem of Erd\H{o}s, Hajnal, and Milner \cite{EHM70}, who proved this is true for $\alpha < \omega_1^{\omega+2}$. In \cite{Er82e} Erd\H{o}s offers \$250 for showing what happens when $\alpha=\omega_1^{\omega+2}$ and \$500 for settling the general case. Larson \cite{La90} proved this is true for all $\alpha<2^{\aleph_0}$ assuming Martin's axiom .




References

[EHM70] Erd\H{o}s, P. and Hajnal, A. and Milner, E. C., Set mappings and polarized partition relations . Combinatorial theory and its applications, I-III (Proc. Colloq., Balatonf\"{u}red, 1969) (1970), 327-363.

[Er82e] Erd\H{o}s, Paul, Some of my favourite problems which recently have been solved . (1982), 59--79.

[La90] Larson, Jean A., Martin's axiom and ordinal graphs: large independent sets or infinite paths . Ann. Pure Appl. Logic (1990), 31-39.
acceptance_criteria: Prove or disprove the exact original statement without changing quantifiers.
Do not add hypotheses, restrict domains, weaken conclusions, or omit subproblems.
Resolve every boundary case and every explicitly numbered task.
Treat ambiguity as a blocking defect rather than choosing a convenient interpretation.

subgoal_graph.summary:
No solution or classification is claimed. The literal statement is blocked at S0 because “infinite path” is undefined: the induced-path interpretation is immediately false for every infinite ordinal, while the repeat-allowed infinite-walk interpretation is trivially true; the double-ray interpretation requires a separate classification. Under the standard ray interpretation, the finite-separator rank theorem, elimination-forest representation, exact forest-antichain equivalence, full-cardinality antichain theorem, and positive result for all infinite initial ordinals are mathematically well supported and independently checkable. The reports’ agreement on these facts is not itself treated as proof. Scout 3’s stronger claim covering every nonzero limit ordinal below ω³ has the highest immediate information value but rests on a comparatively intricate separation lemma that must be audited before use. The highest-priority positive branch is an order-sensitive demand-tree or Cantor-profile gluing theorem compatible with finite-separator decompositions. The highest-priority negative branch searches first for finite-class or matching selector obstructions, then height-two and recursively nested star forests with unbounded finite depth. Componentwise order-type preservation and arbitrary countable simultaneous requirements are retained as refuted principles, since explicit interleaving and star examples invalidate them. The boundary ω₁^(ω+2) remains unresolved; any ray-interpretation counterexample there must have unbounded finite branch lengths and every finite depth tail of full ambient order type. Source portfolio: 

Pasted text

subgoals.claim: [BLOCKING] Normalize the immutable statement without choosing a convenient meaning: specify simple undirected graphs versus other graph conventions; distinguish a one-way simple ray, a two-way double ray, an induced ray, and a repeat-allowed infinite walk; define inherited order type; and treat separately whether 0 is called a limit ordinal.

[PROVED CONDITIONAL BRANCH] Under the induced-path interpretation, the assertion fails for every infinite limit ordinal: the complete graph has no induced infinite path and has no independent set of infinite order type.

[PROVED CONDITIONAL BRANCH] Under the repeat-allowed infinite-walk interpretation, the assertion holds for every ordinal: any edge permits an endlessly alternating walk, so a graph with no such walk is edgeless and its whole vertex set is independent.

[OPEN BRANCH] Classify the statement when “infinite path” means a two-way infinite simple path. Do not import results from the ray branch unless their hypotheses still exclude double rays and preserve the required ordinal order type.

[CONDITIONAL LOCK] For the main proof-search branch, fix a graph to be simple and undirected and an infinite path to be a one-way simple ray of distinct vertices; an independent set of type α is a subset of α with inherited order type exactly α.

[PROVED UNDER S4] Establish the finite-separator rank theorem: a graph is rayless exactly when it belongs to the least hierarchy generated from finite graphs by deleting a finite separator and requiring all remaining components to have smaller rank.

[PROVED UNDER S4] From a finite-separator rank decomposition, construct a rooted forest on the same vertex set, with no infinite branch, in which every edge of the original graph joins comparable vertices. Check explicitly that finite separator bags are replaced by finite chains and that every original edge is covered.

[PROVED UNDER S4] Show that the comparability graph of a rooted forest whose predecessor chains are finite and which has no infinite branch is rayless. The proof must rule out a ray that alternates between different descendant cones rather than assuming it is itself a tree branch.

[PROVED UNDER S4] Deduce the exact equivalence: P(α) holds exactly when every rooted forest on the ordered set α with no infinite branch has an antichain whose inherited ordinal order has type α. Verify both directions and do not relabel a subset without checking preservation of inherited order type.

[PROVED UNDER S4] Prove that every infinite branchless rooted forest has an antichain of the same cardinality as its vertex set, for example by mapping every vertex to a leaf descendant with finite fibers. State the choice principle used to obtain an infinite branch when a node has no leaf descendant.

[PROVED BOUNDARIES] Resolve 0, ω, and all infinite initial ordinals. If 0 is admitted as a limit ordinal, the empty independent set settles it. The case ω and every infinite initial ordinal follow from P1 because a subset of an initial ordinal with full cardinality has full order type.

[PROVED UNDER S4] For an infinite regular cardinal κ and finitely many κ-sized vertex sets in a rayless graph, construct one independent set meeting every specified set in κ vertices. Audit the concentration-versus-diffusion split at a finite separator and the disjoint refinement of finitely many κ-sized component supports.

[PROVED POSITIVE FAMILY] Deduce P(κ·m) for every infinite regular initial ordinal κ and every positive finite m by applying P3 to the consecutive κ-blocks and verifying that κ vertices inside a κ-block have inherited type κ. This includes every ω·m.

[OPEN VERIFICATION] Audit the proposed λ-family refinement theorem for regular infinite cardinals λ≤κ: from λ many κ-sized demands, retain a λ-sized set of indices and meet each retained demand in κ vertices. Check all three separator cases, especially the transfinite greedy choice of distinct components and the disjoint refinement of λ many κ-sized supports.

[CONDITIONAL POSITIVE FAMILY] If P5 is validated, deduce P(κ·λ) for regular infinite initial cardinals λ≤κ, checking that the retained block-index set has inherited order type λ and that the selected part of every retained block has type κ.

[REFUTED PRINCIPLE] Do not require one independent set to meet every member of an arbitrary countable family infinitely. A disjoint union of stars, with one demand consisting of all centers and the remaining demands consisting of individual leaf sets, refutes this strengthening.

[REFUTED PRINCIPLE] Do not infer that replacing every interleaved component by a subset of the same internal order type preserves the order type of the union. The diagonal tail selection in the columns of ω² preserves type ω in every column but collapses the union from type ω² to type ω.

[PROVED REDUCTION] Define the finite-transversal property FT(α): every partition of α into nonempty finite classes has a transversal of type α. Prove that failure of FT(α) yields a rayless counterexample by making each class a clique and placing no edges between classes.

[OPEN HIGH-FALSIFIABILITY BRANCH] Decide first the size-two specialization of B1: whether every matching or fixed-point-free involution on a limit ordinal α admits an independent transversal of inherited type α. Stress-test noninitial ordinals, singular cofinalities, limit exponents, and heavily interleaved pairs.

[PROVED REDUCTION] Characterize antichains in a height-two star forest with roots R, leaves L, and parent map p:L→R: every antichain is contained in S union {ℓ∈L:p(ℓ)∉S} for some S⊆R. Thus a star counterexample is exactly a parent map for which every such root-leaf tradeoff has type below α.

[OPEN CONSTRUCTION BRANCH] Search for recursively nested star forests of finite but unbounded branch length whose labels are nonconvexly interleaved through α. Require an explicit well-founded finite-separator certificate and verify by the recursive antichain profile equation that every antichain has type below α.

[OPEN VERIFICATION, HIGH INFORMATION GAIN] Fully prove or refute Scout 3’s finite-pattern separation lemma for finitely many mandatory infinite sets and finitely many sequences of infinite sets. The audit must verify that concentrated and diffuse requirements can be assigned to components without hidden incompatibilities and that the assembled subsets are pairwise anticomplete.

[CONDITIONAL POSITIVE RANGE] If Q1 survives audit, derive P(α) for every nonzero limit α<ω³ by decomposing α into finitely many ω²-blocks and ω-blocks, retaining infinitely many rows in every required ω²-block, and checking the inherited ordinal sum exactly.

[OPEN BOUNDARY] Resolve α=ω³. Prove a two-level separation theorem retaining infinitely many outer blocks and, inside each retained outer block, infinitely many rows with infinite pairwise anticomplete subsets; or construct a certified branchless forest refuting that theorem and determine whether it also refutes P(ω³).

[PROVED ORDINAL LEMMA] For every ordinal β and every finite coloring of ω^β, prove that one color class has inherited order type ω^β. Check successor and limit exponents and avoid replacing cofinal cardinality by order type.

[PROVED NECESSARY CONDITIONS] At α=ω₁^(ω+2), rewrite α as a power of ω and use D1 with C4 to show that any counterexample forest must have unbounded finite branch lengths, every individual depth level and every finite union of levels must have type below α, and every depth tail must retain type α.

[OPEN POSITIVE BRANCH] Define an order-sensitive hierarchical profile or demand-tree norm for subsets of α, especially α=ω₁^(ω+2), which detects copies of α and records unbounded lower-scale complexity across canonical blocks. It must survive interleaved component replacement and must not imply either refuted principle N1 or N2.

[OPEN MAIN PROOF BRANCH] Using G1, prove a concentration-or-gluing theorem for a finite-separator decomposition: either one lower-rank cone retains the full α-profile, or fronts from sufficiently many incomparable cones can be amalgamated without decreasing the α-profile. Iterating the first alternative must produce a forbidden infinite branch.

[OPEN MAIN COUNTEREXAMPLE BRANCH] If B2, B4, Q3, or the proposed gluing principle G2 fails, extract an explicit ordinally labeled branchless forest or finite-class graph and verify both required negations: it has no ray, and every subset of inherited order type α contains a comparable pair or graph edge.

[AUXILIARY FALSIFICATION PROGRAM] Implement finite block-profile tests for matchings, finite classes, stars, and nested forests. Compute Pareto-optimal antichain profiles recursively, and use the results only to refute candidate gluing laws or identify scalable obstruction patterns, never as proof of the transfinite statement.

[OPEN] Classify exactly the limit ordinals α satisfying the immutable statement under every materially distinct interpretation identified in S0. Under the ray interpretation, complete either G2 or E1, resolve ω³ and ω₁^(ω+2), verify every ordinal-order gluing step, and state honestly if the general classification remains unresolved.

regulator.rationale:
The rejection is caused by fatal artifact-level truncation, not by an identified local proof gap or a demonstrated defect in the validated subgoal graph. The candidate ends before completing its first normalization, contains no substantive mathematical claim, and supplies no dependency chain that can be patched. REVISE_PROOF is therefore insufficient because there is no proof body to repair. REVISE_PLAN is not justified because the reviews do not show that any validated dependency is false, circular, or structurally inadequate. The cheapest valid recovery is to regenerate the candidate from the beginning using the existing DAG, without extrapolating from the missing continuation. 

Pasted text
new_constraints: Discard the truncated candidate completely; do not preserve or reconstruct any unstated intended argument.
Begin by discharging S0 explicitly: separate the ray, double-ray, induced-path, and repeat-allowed infinite-walk interpretations; specify the graph convention; and treat the status of 0 separately.
Do not select a preferred interpretation and present it as the answer to the immutable statement.
Under each nontrivial interpretation, state every definition and imported theorem with exact hypotheses before use.
Follow the validated dependency graph in order and label which nodes are proved, refuted, or remain open.
Do not claim S2 until both directions of the forest equivalence and preservation of inherited ordinal order type are proved.
Do not infer order type from cardinality; every gluing or refinement argument must verify ordinal type in the inherited ordering.
Stress-test all proposed positive principles against finite clique partitions, matchings, height-two stars, nested stars, interleaved components, singular cofinalities, and limit exponents.
Resolve the specified boundary cases, including 0 when applicable, omega, initial ordinals, omega cubed, and omega_1^(omega+2).
The rewritten artifact must end with an explicit classification or an honest statement that the GOAL remains unresolved; it must not present a partial reduction as a solution.
